content.text: Webhooks are HTTP callbacks: when an event occurs, the producer sends an HTTP POST to a URL you register. Agent platforms use webhooks to notify your service when a run completes, a human approves a step, or an external SaaS tool finishes work — all without you ever polling for status.
A webhook is a user-defined HTTP endpoint that a third-party service calls when a specific event happens. Instead of your code repeatedly asking

every few seconds (polling), the provider pushes a signed JSON payload to your endpoint the moment the event fires. Your handler validates the signature, acknowledges receipt quickly with a 2xx response, and processes the payload — often asynchronously in a background job.

In agentic AI systems, webhooks cover the slow-work boundary: an agent run that takes several minutes, a human-in-the-loop approval that may take hours, or an external pipeline (CI build, billing event, data export) whose completion time is unknown. The producer delivers the outcome; your service reacts.

Long agent runs (minutes to hours) do not belong on open HTTP connections. Polling wastes compute and adds latency proportional to the polling interval. Webhooks decouple execution from notification: the agent runtime finishes, then POSTs results to your integration URL. Your service sleeps until notified, then acts.

The same event-driven contract that webhooks enforce is widely used across the modern SaaS ecosystem: Stripe fires webhooks on payment events, GitHub fires them on push and PR events, Slack fires them on message actions, and virtually every major API platform offers webhook delivery for asynchronous notifications. Understanding webhooks lets you connect your agent to all of these without custom polling infrastructure.
— your service reacts when state changes, not on a fixed timer, eliminating unnecessary traffic
— no wasted polling requests; HTTP resources are only used when there is real work to deliver
— producer and consumer run independently; the agent finishes on its schedule and your handler acts on its schedule
— chain multiple systems without shared databases; each system fires its own webhook to the next stage
— reuse TLS, auth headers, load balancers, and observability tooling already in your stack
— notification arrives within seconds of the event, not after your next polling interval
— at-least-once delivery is the norm; your handler must be idempotent because duplicate deliveries are possible
— the producer needs a reachable URL; local development requires tunnels like ngrok or smee.io
— events may arrive out of order, especially under retries; use sequence numbers or timestamps to detect this
— asynchronous delivery is harder to trace than synchronous API calls; delivery logs and request inspection tools are essential
— a public POST endpoint is a potential target; you must verify signatures and reject replayed or forged payloads

You configure a callback URL in the agent platform or SaaS admin UI. Some systems require a challenge handshake at registration time: the provider sends a GET request with a token in the query string and expects your endpoint to echo it back, proving you control the URL. Once verified, the provider stores your URL and begins delivering events.
When an event fires, the producer sends an HTTP POST to your URL with a JSON body and a signature header such as
. If your endpoint returns a non-2xx response or times out, the provider retries with exponential backoff — commonly over a 72-hour window. Return
quickly (within the provider's timeout, typically 10–30 seconds). Do not block on downstream processing; enqueue instead.
Most providers include an HMAC-SHA256 signature over the raw request body using a shared secret. The receiver recomputes the HMAC and compares it using a constant-time comparison function (
) to prevent timing attacks. Many providers also include a timestamp in the payload or a header; rejecting payloads older than five minutes prevents replay attacks.

Because at-least-once delivery means duplicates can arrive, every handler must be idempotent. The standard approach: record the event ID in a database or cache on first receipt; if a duplicate arrives, return 200 immediately without re-processing. Set a TTL on the deduplication record matching or exceeding the provider's retry window.

attrs.alt: Webhooks architecture diagram showing event producer, webhook endpoint, message queue, and background worker

content.text: A production webhook stack has three tiers. The
(agent platform, CI/CD system, SaaS API) fires signed HTTP POST requests with exponential backoff on failure. The
validates the HMAC signature, rejects stale timestamps, deduplicates by event ID, enqueues the payload, and returns 200 within 200ms. The
processes the event idempotently — indexing results, triggering downstream agents, or notifying users. Returning 401 or 400 stops retries permanently; returning 5xx causes the provider to retry.
for async agent run completion, billing events, CI/CD pipeline outcomes, and human-approval callbacks
when work takes longer than 30 seconds and holding an open HTTP connection is unreliable
when you cannot expose a public URL (local development) or when the event producer does not support webhooks
for live UI updates to a connected browser — not server-to-server async notification
for high-frequency real-time data (hundreds of events per second) — prefer a streaming protocol instead

The implementation has two layers: signature verification on the raw request body, and idempotent asynchronous processing. Use FastAPI with a background task queue. Always verify the signature before parsing the JSON body, and always respond within the provider's timeout window.

# webhook_handler.py — FastAPI webhook receiver with signature validation

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
import hmac
import hashlib
import time
import json
import asyncio
from typing import Any

app = FastAPI()
WEBHOOK_SECRET = "whsec_your_secret_here"  # load from env in production
MAX_TIMESTAMP_SKEW_SECONDS = 300  # 5-minute replay window


def verify_signature(body: bytes, sig_header: str | None, ts_header: str | None) -> None:
    """Raise HTTPException if signature is missing, invalid, or replayed."""
    if not sig_header:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="missing X-Signature-256")

    # Reject stale timestamps to block replay attacks
    if ts_header:
        try:
            ts = int(ts_header)
            if abs(time.time() - ts) > MAX_TIMESTAMP_SKEW_SECONDS:
                raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="timestamp too old")
        except ValueError:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="invalid timestamp")

    # Compute expected HMAC over raw body bytes
    expected = hmac.new(
        WEBHOOK_SECRET.encode(),
        body,
        hashlib.sha256,
    ).hexdigest()

    # Constant-time comparison prevents timing-based signature guessing
    if not hmac.compare_digest(f"sha256={expected}", sig_header):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="invalid signature")


async def process_agent_completion(event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    """Background task: process agent result after acknowledging receipt."""
    run_id = event.get("run_id")
    status = event.get("status")
    output_url = event.get("output_url")

    # Simulate downstream work: index, store, notify
    print(f"[worker] processing run {run_id}, status={status}, output={output_url}")
    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield to event loop

    # In production: fetch output_url, index content, update database, etc.


@app.post("/webhooks/agent-complete")
async def agent_complete(
    request: Request,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
) -> dict[str, bool]:
    body = await request.body()
    verify_signature(
        body,
        request.headers.get("X-Signature-256"),
        request.headers.get("X-Webhook-Timestamp"),
    )
    event = json.loads(body)

    # Enqueue heavy work; return 200 immediately to prevent retries
    background_tasks.add_task(process_agent_completion, event)
    return {"received": True}

Because at-least-once delivery is the norm, you must protect against double-processing. Store event IDs in Redis with an expiry matching or slightly exceeding the provider's retry window. On duplicate delivery, acknowledge with 200 and return without re-running the business logic.

# idempotency.py — Redis-backed event deduplication

import redis.asyncio as redis
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
import json

app = FastAPI()
r = redis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379", decode_responses=True)

DEDUP_TTL_SECONDS = 72 * 3600  # match provider's retry window


async def already_processed(event_id: str) -> bool:
    """Return True and mark as seen, or False if first delivery."""
    # SET NX (set if not exists) is atomic — safe under concurrent delivery
    key = f"webhook:seen:{event_id}"
    was_new = await r.set(key, "1", ex=DEDUP_TTL_SECONDS, nx=True)
    return was_new is None  # None means key already existed → duplicate


async def handle_event(event: dict) -> None:
    event_id = event.get("id", "")
    if not event_id:
        return  # malformed — should be rejected earlier

    if await already_processed(event_id):
        print(f"[dedup] skipping duplicate event {event_id}")
        return

    event_type = event.get("type", "unknown")
    if event_type == "agent.run.completed":
        await process_run_completed(event["data"])
    elif event_type == "agent.run.failed":
        await process_run_failed(event["data"])
    else:
        print(f"[handler] unhandled event type: {event_type}")


async def process_run_completed(data: dict) -> None:
    print(f"[handler] run {data['run_id']} completed — indexing output")
    # ... downstream logic here ...


async def process_run_failed(data: dict) -> None:
    print(f"[handler] run {data['run_id']} failed — alerting team")
    # ... alert + retry logic here ...


@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def receive_event(
    request: Request,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
) -> dict:
    body = await request.body()
    # (signature verification omitted for brevity — always include in production)
    event = json.loads(body)
    background_tasks.add_task(handle_event, event)
    return {"ok": True}

Webhooks require a publicly reachable URL. During local development, use a tunnel such as ngrok, smee.io, or the GitHub CLI's built-in proxy. The tunnel forwards incoming HTTP requests to your local server.

# Terminal 1: start your FastAPI server
uvicorn webhook_handler:app --reload --port 8000

# Terminal 2: start ngrok tunnel
ngrok http 8000
# Outputs: Forwarding https://abc123.ngrok.io -> http://localhost:8000

# Register this URL in your webhook provider's admin UI:
#   https://abc123.ngrok.io/webhooks/agent-complete

# Test with curl (replace secret and body as appropriate):
SECRET="whsec_your_secret_here"
BODY='{"id":"evt_test_001","type":"agent.run.completed","data":{"run_id":"run_abc"}}'
SIG=$(echo -n "$BODY" | openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "$SECRET" | awk '{print $2}')
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/webhooks/agent-complete \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-Signature-256: sha256=$SIG" \
  -d "$BODY"

A legal-tech company runs a nightly batch agent that analyzes 10,000 documents using an LLM. Each document job runs on a vendor cloud and takes two to five minutes. When a job finishes, the vendor POSTs to

with the run ID, status, and a signed URL to fetch the output. The webhook handler verifies the signature and enqueues a Celery task within 150ms. A separate worker fetches the output, runs a secondary classification pass, indexes the result into Elasticsearch, and marks the document record as done. Failed deliveries from the vendor retry for 72 hours; Redis-based idempotency keys prevent double-indexing on retry.
The same company uses GitHub webhooks for their CI pipeline: on every push to the agent's prompt configuration repository, GitHub fires a
event. The webhook receiver validates the
header, parses the commit diff, and triggers a re-evaluation run against a held-out evaluation dataset. Results are posted back as a PR comment using the GitHub REST API.
— providers typically wait 10–30 seconds for a response; anything slower triggers a retry and potential duplicate processing
— any attacker who discovers your URL can POST fake agent completions; always verify HMAC before trusting the body
— even with a valid signature, verify that run_id exists in your database before acting on it
— a 5xx causes the provider to retry, which replays side effects already applied; use idempotent steps or transactional outbox
— JSON re-serialization can change key order or whitespace, making the HMAC mismatch; always compute on the raw bytes
— without event ID tracking, a provider retry on network error sends the event twice, potentially billing a user twice or triggering two agent runs
— stack traces in 500 responses leak implementation details to the provider's logs; return a generic error message
Verify HMAC on raw body bytes
using hmac.compare_digest for constant-time comparison; re-run your framework's body buffering before the route reads it
; enqueue all heavy work to a background worker or message queue — do not process in-line
with TTL matching or exceeding the provider's retry window (commonly 72 hours)
— compare the timestamp in the header against server time; deny payloads outside a five-minute skew window
that re-processes an event from the provider's delivery log when a worker failure is detected after the fact
; rotate webhook secrets periodically and revoke the old secret after allowing a brief overlap window for in-flight deliveries
with event ID, type, and receipt timestamp — this is invaluable for debugging delivery gaps and auditing integrations

Webhook endpoints are CPU-light (HMAC computation is cheap) and IO-light (a single Redis SET and a queue push). A single FastAPI instance with an async event loop handles thousands of concurrent deliveries without issue. The bottleneck is almost always the downstream worker, not the endpoint itself.
— scale the webhook receiver and the background processor independently; receivers are stateless and scale horizontally with no coordination
— Redis with persistence (AOF), AWS SQS, or RabbitMQ ensures events survive a worker crash; in-memory queues lose events on restart
— webhook receivers benefit from autoscaling; during traffic spikes the queue absorbs load and workers drain it at their own rate
— configure a DLQ for events that fail processing after N retries; inspect these for bugs rather than silently dropping them
— if your Redis or queue is slow, provider delivery timeouts create retries that compound into a feedback loop
— tunnels local ports to a public URL; essential for local development and integration testing of webhook handlers
— GitHub's open-source webhook proxy; replayable and inspectable with a simple CLI client
— managed webhook infrastructure that handles signing, retries, delivery logs, and portal for end users; open-source core available
— managed webhook gateway with retry control, filtering, transformations, and rate limiting; reduces endpoint boilerplate
— the most common Python background task stack; Celery workers consume from Redis queues with configurable concurrency and retry logic
— serverless alternative: the webhook endpoint writes to SQS, and Lambda triggers automatically per message batch; scales to zero at rest
— emerging specification for consistent webhook signature formats across providers; reduces per-provider integration code
Webhooks are the right tool when the event originates
your control (third-party platform) and the work is asynchronous. SSE and WebSockets are for
where a browser has an open connection. Message queues are for internal service-to-service communication inside your own infrastructure.
Always verify the HMAC-SHA256 signature over the raw request body using your shared secret. Use

— to prevent timing oracle attacks. Reject any payload whose embedded timestamp is outside a five-minute window of server time. This prevents an attacker who captures a valid POST from replaying it hours later.
— plain HTTP exposes payloads on the network; many providers reject non-TLS URLs outright
separately from your public API — attackers may probe your signature endpoint with high request volume
when the provider publishes a stable IP range; blocks unsolicited POSTs before they reach signature verification
Log payload hashes, not full secrets or PII
— webhook logs are often shared across teams; avoid leaking sensitive data
— set an alert if no webhooks arrive for longer than your expected maximum event interval; this signals a misconfigured URL or a provider outage
— return 503 from your endpoint and confirm the provider retries; this catches providers that give up after a single failure
— support both old and new secrets simultaneously for a brief overlap window so in-flight deliveries signed with the old key are not rejected

Webhooks are a doorbell notification: the agent runtime rings your doorbell when it finishes a job. You answer quickly — just a 200 OK — and handle the actual package (the result) inside at your own pace. You do not stand at the door for twenty minutes while the agent works. The doorbell (the webhook) and the work it triggers (the background task) are deliberately separated. If you take too long to answer, the delivery driver (the provider) assumes you are out and tries again later.

The hardest part of webhooks is not the HTTP — it is the operational discipline: verify everything, respond fast, assume duplicates, and monitor for silence. Get these four habits right and webhooks become one of the most reliable integration primitives in distributed systems.
— the producer calls your registered URL when an event fires; no polling required
Verify HMAC signatures on raw bytes
using constant-time comparison; reject stale timestamps to prevent replay attacks
Respond within the timeout (usually under 10 seconds)
; enqueue slow work to a background worker immediately after acknowledgement
— store event IDs in a cache with a TTL matching the retry window and skip already-processed events
— under retries, a later event may arrive before an earlier one; use sequence numbers or timestamps to detect this
Use a durable queue between endpoint and worker
— in-memory task dispatch loses events on restart; Redis or SQS-backed queues survive failures
Webhooks are ideal for agent run completion and third-party integrations
— not a substitute for live streaming to connected browser clients
